questions.question: What is the difference between named exports and default exports in ES modules?
questions.answer: Named exports: a module can have multiple named exports (export const foo = ...; export function bar() {}). Imported with exact name in braces: import { foo, bar } from "./module". Can be renamed on import: import { foo as baz }. Default export: one per module (export default class App). Imported without braces: import App from "./App". Can be imported with any name. Best practice: use named exports for utilities (easy to tree-shake, refactor-safe); use default exports for React components (convention).

questions.question: What is circular dependency? How do you fix it?
questions.answer: A circular dependency occurs when Module A imports Module B which imports Module A. This creates infinite loading cycles. Symptoms: undefined imports, unexpected behavior. Fixes: (1) Extract shared code into a third module that both import from. (2) Move the import inside the function that needs it (lazy evaluation). (3) Restructure to remove the cycle — often the cycle indicates modules with mixed responsibilities.

questions.question: What is tree shaking?
questions.answer: Tree shaking is a build optimization where the bundler (Webpack, Rollup, Vite) statically analyzes ES module imports and removes unused exports from the final bundle. It works because ES module imports are static — the bundler knows at build time exactly which exports are used. CommonJS require() is dynamic (require(condition ? "a" : "b")) so it can't be tree-shaken. This is one reason to prefer ESM over CommonJS for libraries.

questions.question: What are the performance characteristics of high-frequency Import Statements queries?
questions.answer: It depends on the data structure used. Average queries execute in O(1) time when using hash indexes, but can degrade to O(N) linear search if colliding.
